facts.claim: The 2008 financial crisis began with the collapse of the U.S. housing bubble and subprime mortgage market, triggering the worst global downturn since the Great Depression.

Years of loose mortgage lending inflated home prices; when prices stopped rising, borrowers defaulted, the mortgage-backed securities built on those loans lost value, and the losses spread through the banking system.
facts.notes: The cause-effect chain — used for the flow-diagram-scene.

facts.claim: Lehman Brothers filed for bankruptcy on September 15, 2008, with $639 billion in assets — the largest bankruptcy filing in U.S. history.
The Dow Jones Industrial Average fell about 54%, from a peak of 14,164.53 on October 9, 2007, to a low of 6,547.05 on March 9, 2009.
Congress authorized $700 billion for the Troubled Asset Relief Program (TARP) in October 2008 to stabilize the financial system.
TARP's final lifetime cost to taxpayers was about $31 billion, a small fraction of the $700 billion originally authorized.
The government's rescue of AIG totaled about $182 billion, combining Treasury and Federal Reserve commitments.
The AIG bailout was later fully recovered, with the government realizing a $22.7 billion positive return for taxpayers.
U.S. unemployment peaked at 10.0% in October 2009, roughly double the 5% rate from December 2007.
Regulators closed 414 U.S. banks between 2008 and 2011 (25 in 2008, 140 in 2009, 157 in 2010, 92 in 2011).
